coredata.dc:title: Delaminating Ti3C2 MXene by blossom of ZnIn2S4 microflowers for noble-metal-free photocatalytic hydrogen production
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Weixin
Li, Zhipeng
Wu, Chao
Zhang, Hanjie
Sun, Jie
Li, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel strategy was exploited to achieve the delamination of Ti3C2 MXene multilayers into ultrathin flakes by blossom of ZnIn2S4 microflowers via a one-pot solvothermal method. There is no need to peel off the MXene bulk ahead of its combination with the semiconductor. The obtained ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 binary composites were applied for visible-light-driven photocatalytic hydrogen production without noble metal cocatalyst, and the optimized sample exhibited a hydrogen-production efficiency of 978.7 μmol h − 1 g − 1 with the corresponding apparent quantum efficiency of 24.2% at 420 nm, which was 2.7 times higher than bare ZnIn2S4. Through the comprehensive analysis based on spectroscopy measurements, electrochemical techniques and energy band theory, such enhancement was mainly attributed to (1) the highly-exposed surface that was beneficial for the adequate exposure of reactive sites and (2) the intimate contact interface that favored the transfer of photogenerated carriers. This study provides a new way of thinking for synthesizing ultrathin MXene-based composite materials for noble-metal-free and highly-efficient photocatalysis applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnIn2S4
Ti3C2
MXene
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen production

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic activity

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) the Ti3AlC2, Ti3C2 and (b) the prepared ZnIn2S4 and ZTx samples. (c) Raman spectra of the ZnIn2S4, ZT10 and Ti3C2 samples.
(a) FESEM and (b) HRTEM images of the prepared ZT10 sample. (c) HAADF-STEM image of the ZT10 sample and the EDS mapping images of its Zn, In, S, and Ti atoms.
FESEM images of the ZT10 sample with the solvothermal time for (a) 10 min, (b) 1 h, and (c) 12 h, respectively. (d) FESEM image of the ZT80 sample.
(a) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and (b) the corresponding pore size distribution curves of the prepared ZnIn2S4 and ZTx samples.

Photographs of (a) the automatic online trace gas analysis system (Labsolar-6A, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.) and (b) the multi-channel photochemical reaction system (PCX-50B, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.). (c) Dependence of the amount of H2 production on the concentration of the ZT10 sample in the system. (d) Comparison of the photocatalytic H2 production rates of the prepared samples.

(a) Steady-state PL spectra of the ZnIn2S4, ZT5, ZT10, ZT40 and ZT80 samples and (inset) its local magnification. (b) Time-resolved transient photoluminescence decay of the ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 samples. (c) TPR plots and (d) EIS plots of the ZnIn2S4, ZT5, ZT10, ZT40 and ZT80 samples.
High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) C 1 s, (b) Ti 2p, and (c) Zn 2p of the ZnIn2S4, ZT10 and Ti3C2 samples.

(a) DRS spectra of the prepared samples and (b) corresponding Tauc plots of ZnIn2S4 and ZT10. Mott-Schottky plots of (c) ZnIn2S4 and (d) ZT10 at a frequency of 1000 Hz, 2000 Hz and 3000 Hz in Na2SO4 (0.5 M). (e) CPD values of the Ti3C2, ZT10, and ZnIn2S4 samples. (f) LSV curves of the ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 samples.
Proposed photocatalytic H2 evolution mechanism over ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composite photocatalysts.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process of the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites.
Actual Ti3C2 to ZnIn2S4 ratios in the composite and comparison of photocatalytic H2 production performance of the prepared samples.

row.entry: Actual molar ratio of Ti3C2 to ZnIn2S4 (%)
Mass of sample for HER a (mg)
R H2 per mass (μmol h − 1 g − 1)
R H2 per A BET (μmol h − 1 m − 2)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: HER is the abbreviation of H2 evolution reaction.

head.ce:title: Delaminating Ti3C2 MXene by blossom of ZnIn2S4 microflowers for noble-metal-free photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Energy Materials Chemistry of Ministry of Education & Hubei Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Materials Science, South-Central Minzu University, Wuhan, 430074, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Energy Materials Chemistry of Ministry of Education & Hubei Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Energy Materials Chemistry of Ministry of Education & Hubei Key Laboratory of Catalysis and Materials Science, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan, 430074, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors equally contribute to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel strategy was exploited to achieve the delamination of Ti3C2 MXene multilayers into ultrathin flakes by blossom of ZnIn2S4 microflowers via a one-pot solvothermal method. There is no need to peel off the MXene bulk ahead of its combination with the semiconductor. The obtained ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 binary composites were applied for visible-light-driven photocatalytic hydrogen production without noble metal cocatalyst, and the optimized sample exhibited a hydrogen-production efficiency of 978.7 μmol h − 1 g − 1 with the corresponding apparent quantum efficiency of 24.2% at 420 nm, which was 2.7 times higher than bare ZnIn2S4. Through the comprehensive analysis based on spectroscopy measurements, electrochemical techniques and energy band theory, such enhancement was mainly attributed to (1) the highly-exposed surface that was beneficial for the adequate exposure of reactive sites and (2) the intimate contact interface that favored the transfer of photogenerated carriers. This study provides a new way of thinking for synthesizing ultrathin MXene-based composite materials for noble-metal-free and highly-efficient photocatalysis applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic hydrogen (H2) production is momentous for the development of green solar energy conversion technology and the achievement of carbon neutral [1–6]. Hitherto, diverse semiconductors, especially visible-light-responsive ones, have been extensively explored for highly-efficient photocatalytic H2 production [7–10]. Among them, hexagonal ZnIn2S4 stands out to catch growing attention owing to its appropriate band gap, unique electronic structure and distinctive textural properties [11–14]. It usually exists in the form of nanosheets or self-assembled flower-like microspheres [15–19]. However, bare ZnIn2S4 usually suffers from rapid photogenerated carrier recombination and photocorrosion [20–22]. One of the effective strategies for addressing this issue is to decorate cocatalyst with a larger work function to form Schottky barriers, which can effectively inhibit the recombination of light-induced electron-hole pairs and prolong their lifetime. For ZnIn2S4-based photocatalysts, the cocatalyst candidates usually include noble metals (Pt, Au) and carbon materials (graphene, carbon quantum dots, carbon nanotubes) [23–27].

In 2011, Naguib et al. successfully extracted the Al layers from Ti3AlC2 MAX phase by hydrofluoric acid (HF) to form a novel laminated Ti3C2 nanocrystal, which was named as “MXene” to highlight its graphene-like properties and morphology [28]. Thereafter, more transition metal carbides and/or nitrides were discovered to greatly expand this burgeoning 2D family of MXenes [29]. The general formulas of MAX and MXene are M n
+1AX n and M n
+1X n , respectively, in which M refers to an early transition metal (such as Ti, Nb, Ta, and Mo), A is an element of main group III and IV, X is carbon and/or nitrogen, and n = 1–3. The resulted MXene product possesses a unique accordion-like multilayer structure, with abundant functional terminations such as –F, –O and –OH on the surface. Therefore, Ti3C2 MXene is naturally hydrophilic and tends to tightly couple with other materials [30–33]. Besides, ultrathin Ti3C2 MXene nanosheets can be easily received from the Ti3C2 multilayers by conventional delamination strategies, such as ultrasonication and intercalation, which largely expands its application scope [34, 35]. More importantly, similar to noble metals and carbon materials, Ti3C2 possesses excellent electrical conductivity and suitable Fermi level (E F) to form Schottky-type heterostructures with n-type semiconductors, facilitating the separation of the photoinduced electrons and holes [36–38]. Consequently, Ti3C2 owns extensive application prospects as a cocatalyst in photocatalysis [36, 39-41].

Indeed, Ti3C2 has been identified to be a useful cocatalyst to significantly promote the photocatalytic performance of ZnIn2S4. For instance, Zuo et al. anchored ultrathin ZnIn2S4 nanosheets on single-layered Ti3C2 for photocatalytic H2 evolution [42]. Li et al. synthesized a 2D/2D ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 Schottky heterojunction for pollutant degradation [43]. Ren et al. loaded ZnIn2S4 nanosheets on the surface of the monolayer Ti3C2-composed hollow microspheres, achieving efficient visible-light photocatalytic Cr (VI) reduction [44]. It is obvious that more and more attention has been paid to the 2D materials in the field of photocatalysis [45]. Similarly, in most cases, single- or few-layered Ti3C2 nanosheets were also adopted as the cocatalyst instead of multilayered Ti3C2 owing to their large surface area, versatile platform, and ample exposed metal sites. Nevertheless, in the previous studies on the Ti3C2 nanosheets-based composite photocatalysts, the delamination process of multilayered Ti3C2 must be completed in advance, which was usually cumbersome and energy-wasting with an unsatisfactory yield, along with the great possibility of being oxidized [28, 46, 47].

Herein, a novel and facile strategy was exploited to achieve the delamination of Ti3C2 MXene multilayers into ultrathin flakes by the blossom of ZnIn2S4 microflowers during the solvothermal treatment process. Typically, thiacetamide (TAA) served as both an intercalator of the multilayered Ti3C2 and a sulfur source of ZnIn2S4. As illustrated in Scheme 1, in the solvothermal environment, ZnIn2S4 first seeded between the interlayers of Ti3C2 MXene and gradually grew up to be nanosheets, and then further bloomed to be flower-like microspheres to puff the Ti3C2 layers. At last, ultrathin Ti3C2 nanosheets were received and interspersed between the ZnIn2S4 flowers. In the obtained ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites, the intimate contact interface was formed and favored the transfer of photogenerated carriers, and the highly-exposed surface of both Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 was beneficial for the adequate exposure of reactive sites. In consequence, without the assistance of any noble metal cocatalyst, the prepared binary composite exhibited an undeniably higher photocatalytic H2 production performance than pristine ZnIn2S4. To our knowledge, no previous literature regarding Ti3C2 MXene-based composite photocatalysts has been reported to achieve the delamination and combination processes in one step.

Ti3AlC2 MAX phase (Forsman, 98%), hydrofluoric acid (HF, Sinopharm, ≥ 40%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, Sinopharm, ≥ 98%), indium chloride hydrate (InCl3·4H2O, Aladdin, 99.9%), TAA (C2H5NS, Sinopharm, ≥ 99.0%) and ethyl alcohol (C2H5OH, Sinopharm, ≥ 99.7%) were used directly as received.

Ti3AlC2 powder (1 g) was added into 20 mL of HF aqueous solution at a very slow rate. Then, the obtained suspension was stirred for 30 min at ambient temperatures and subsequently transferred into a 35 °C water bath to stir for 24 h. After natural cooling, the powder was vacuumed filtration and washed with deionized (DI) water till pH ≥ 6, and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h, Thereby, the multilayered Ti3C2 MXene was obtained and stored at −20 °C for standby.

ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites were prepared by a solvothermal method in one pot. Typically, 300 mg of the multilayered Ti3C2 MXene was evenly dispersed in 30 mL of DI water. After adding 750 mg of TAA, the mixed suspension was stirred at 450 r/min at room temperature for 2 h, and then treated under ultrasonication for 2 h. During the ultrasonication process, ice bags were used to maintain the water temperature at about 20 °C to prevent possible oxidation of Ti3C2. Subsequently, 1.7 mL of the above Ti3C2-TAA mixture solution (containing 0.1 mmol Ti3C2) was mixed thoroughly with 16.3 mL of DI water and 9 mL of ethanol, and then InCl3·4H2O (2 mmol, 0.5865 g), ZnCl2 (1 mmol, 0.1363 g) and TAA (8 mmol, 0.6010 g) were added successively into the solution. The suspension was stirred for 1 h and ultrasonicated for 10 min at ambient temperature, and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon liner with a stainless steel autoclave, and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the sediment was alternatingly washed with DI water and ethanol for several times. Finally, the collected solid was dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites. For surveying the influence of the Ti3C2 amount on the properties of the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites, before transferring into the Teflon liner, different volume (V = 0.85, 1.7, 3.4, 6.8, and 13.6 mL, respectively) of the Ti3C2-TAA mixture solution was mixed with water ((18–V) mL) and ethanol (9 mL) to maintain the total volume of the reaction solution as 27 mL, and the obtained series of the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites were denoted as ZTx (x = 5, 10, 20, 40, and 80, respectively), where x refers to the mole percent of Ti3C2 to ZnIn2S4 (unit: mol%). Bare ZnIn2S4 was prepared under the same solvothermal condition without adding the Ti3C2-TAA mixture solution. For comparison, Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 with a molar ratio of 10% were physically mixed for photocatalytic H2 production, which was named as pm-ZT10 for short.

The crystallographic information was characterized by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker), using a monochromatic Cu Kα generator (λ = 1.5406 Å). Raman spectra were performed on a laser confocal Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, DXR 2xi). The actual mole percent of Ti3C2 to ZnIn2S4 in the samples was calculated by the test results from inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Prodigy 7 spectrometer, Leeman Labs Inc.). Morphologies of the samples were observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, SU8010, Hitachi) and high angle annular dark-field scanning/transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM, Talos F200S, Thermo Scientific). The element compositions of samples were examined by energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, attached to HAADF-STEM). Zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, Zetasize Nano-ZS90) was carried out to measure the surface charge of the Ti3C2. To obtain Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface areas (A BET) of the materials, nitrogen (N2) adsorption-desorption isotherms were recorded by nitrogen adsorption apparatus (ASAP2020, Micromeritics). The A BET of the materials was calculated by a multipoint BET method in the relative pressure range of 0.05−0.3. The corresponding pore size distribution curves were obtained by the adsorption isotherm. The degassing temperature was set as 175 °C. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained from a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi). The excitation wavelength was 260 nm. Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra were measured under excitation of 375 nm fs pulses. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were tested on Escalab 250xi spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific). Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV−vis DRS) were tested on a UV−vis spectrophotometer (UV2550, Shimadzu). Kelvin probe apparatus (Instytut Fotonowy, Poland) was used to detect the contact potential difference for calculating the surface work function.

Photocatalytic H2-production reaction was carried out in an automatic online trace gas analysis system (Labsolar-6A, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co., Ltd.). The light source is a 300 W Xenon arc lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter, which was positioned 9 cm away from the top of the liquid, and the irradiation area was 33 cm2. Before the H2 production reaction, the photocatalyst sample was evenly dispersed in 80 mL of triethanolamine (TEOA) aqueous solution (10 v%) under ultrasonication, where the TEOA served as the sacrificial agent to consume the photoexcited holes. For photocatalytic H2 production, 0.6 mL of gas was automatically extracted and injected into a gas chromatograph (GC-2018, Shimazu, TCD, with N2 as the carrier gas, 5 Å molecular sieve column) after one hour of illumination, and the photocatalytic H2 production rate was calculated based on the average value of four-hour H2 production amount [24].

The determination of the apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for H2 production was performed in the above system under the illumination of four LEDs (3 W, 420 nm). The LEDs were located on the top of the reactor, 9 cm away from the top of the liquid, and the light intensity on the reactor for each of them was ca. 6.0 mW cm−2 over an area of 1 cm2 [24].

All the photoelectrochemical tests including transient photocurrent responses (TPR), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Mott-Schottky plots and linear sweep voltammetry curves (LSV) were carried out on an electrochemical workstation (CHI760e Instruments) in a standard three-electrode model, utilizing the Ag/AgCl (in saturated KCl solution) and platinum-wire electrode as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Na2SO4 (0.5 M) aqueous solution (pH = 5.45) was served as the electrolyte, and a 420 nm-LED was implemented as the light source when it is needed. For the TPR, EIS, and LSV measurements, the working electrode was prepared by coating the sample slurry on a 0.9 cm × 1.1 cm F-doped SnO2-coated glass (FTO glass) electrode as reported before [48]. For the test of Mott-Schottky plots, the working electrode was prepared by dripping the sample slurry on a glassy carbon electrode.

The phase structures and components of the samples were identified by XRD, Raman, and ICP-OES spectra. Firstly, Fig. 1 (a) shows that the (104) diffraction peak of Ti3AlC2 at 39.0° disappeared in the XRD pattern of the multilayered Ti3C2 MXene, and the (004) and (002) diffraction peaks of Ti3AlC2 at 19.0° and 9.4° shifted to 18.3° and 8.9°, respectively. The comparison result indicated that the Al layer of Ti3AlC2 was removed and Ti3C2 MXene with a larger interlayer spacing was successfully obtained by HF etching [49]. It is worth noting that the peaks at 36.0° and 41.6° are assigned to the impurity of TiC, which was often used as raw material in the fabrication of Ti3AlC2 and was difficult to remove by acid etching [35]. In Fig. 1(b), the XRD pattern of the bare ZnIn2S4 sample matches well with that of standard hexagonal ZnIn2S4 (JCPDS No. 65–2023), and the presence of Ti3C2 in the ZTx composites did not influence the crystallographic structure of ZnIn2S4. In the XRD patterns of the ZT5 and ZT10 samples, no diffraction peaks of Ti3C2 were observed due to its low loading amount and good dispersion. Nevertheless, the existence of Ti3C2 in the composites can be confirmed by Raman spectra (Fig. 1(c)). Taking the Raman spectrum of the ZT10 sample as an example, one can not only observe the characteristic Raman peak at 342 cm−1 for ZnIn2S4, but also find that at 616 cm−1 originated from ω4 Raman-active phonon vibration mode belonging to Ti3C2 [50], as well as those at 1423 and 1570 cm−1 for the D and G bands of Ti3C2, respectively [51, 52]. Besides, according to the ICP-OES results as shown in Table 1 , the actual molar ratios of Ti3C2 to ZnIn2S4 in the ZTx (x = 5, 10, 20, 40, and 80, respectively) samples were calculated to be 3.8%, 6.6%, 13.2%, 24.5%, and 56.8%, respectively. The result once again verified the successful combination of Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 during the solvothermal process. In addition, it should be noted in Fig. 1(b) that the XRD peaks of TiC impurity became apparent with increasing amount of Ti3C2 in the ZTx composites, which was bound to happen. Apart from that, the diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 at 25.4° and 37.9° also gradually appeared in the ZTx when x > 20, indicating that Ti3C2 will inevitably be oxidized to some extent in the solvothermal process.

The morphologies and microstructures of the prepared samples were visually observed by electron microscopes including FESEM and HAADF-STEM. The FESEM image of Ti3C2 (Fig. S1) shows an accordion-like multilayer structure, indicating of the successful Al removal from Ti3AlC2 after the HF etching. This outcome was well consistent with the XRD result in Fig. 1(a). Interestingly, after the combination of Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 with a suitable molar ratio (10%) via the one-pot solvothermal method, the multilayered Ti3C2 was exfoliated into ultrathin nanosheets and interspersed between the flower-like microspheres, which can be seen in the FESEM image of the ZT10 sample (Fig. 2 (a)). Its HRTEM image (Fig. 2(b)) shows obvious lattice fringes with the measured spacing of 0.23, 0.27, and 0.32 nm, corresponding to the Ti3C2 (103), Ti3C2 (0110), and ZnIn2S4 (102) crystal planes, respectively [42, 53], which substantiated the coexistence and tight integration of Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4. Furthermore, the HAADF-STEM image (Fig. 2(c)) of the ZT10 sample exhibited similar morphology to the SEM image (Fig. 2(a)), and its EDS elemental mapping images of the Zn, In, S, and Ti atoms reinforced that it is the Ti3C2 nanoflakes that crossed in the middle of the ZnIn2S4 microspheres.

In order to explore the forming process of the binary ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites, the solvothermal duration time was controlled and the microstructures of the corresponding products were scrutinized. Within the solvothermal treatment for only 10 min, merely a small quantity of ZnIn2S4 nanoflakes grew in situ on the surface of the multilayered Ti3C2 (Fig. 3 (a)). When the solvothermal time extended to 1 h, the Ti3C2 still exhibited in a multilayer form, but larger ZnIn2S4 nanoflakes were formed on its surface (Fig. 3(b)). Surprisingly, when the solvothermal time reached 12 h, most of the multilayer bulks disappeared, and it can be observed that the ZnIn2S4 microflowers were formed, and numerous ultrathin Ti3C2 nanoflakes with an extremely smooth surface emerged between them (Fig. 3(c)). Accordingly, the formation mechanism of the 3D/2D ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites was speculated as illustrated in Scheme 1.

Typically, considering the negative charge on the surface of the multilayered Ti3C2 (see Fig. S2, the Zeta potential of Ti3C2 was tested to be −25.3 mV), Zn2+ and In3+ ions prefer to rapidly adsorb onto the surface and into the interlayers of the Ti3C2 due to the electrostatic attraction. Simultaneously, as a typical intercalator of Ti3C2, TAA molecules will gradually diffuse into the interlayer of Ti3C2, and release S2− slowly during the solvothermal process [54]. Therefore, it is reasonable to state that the growth and blossom of ZnIn2S4 microflowers occurred between the layers of Ti3C2, and lead to its delamination into ultrathin nanoflakes. More importantly, the pyrolysis of the TAA in the solution will bring about a reducing atmosphere (H2S or NH3) in the enclosed reactor [55]. Therefore, the peeled Ti3C2 nanosheets were protected from oxidation and possess a smooth surface (Fig. 2(a) and Fig. 3(c)).

However, when the Ti3C2 was overloaded in the composites, the amount of the precursor of ZnIn2S4 was not enough to peel off all the Ti3C2 multilayers. Therefore, it can be seen from the SEM image of the ZT80 sample (Fig. 3(d)) that some accordion-like multilayers still exist beside the microflowers and nanosheets, and its magnification image (Fig. S3) displays that some nanoparticles were formed on the surface of the Ti3C2 multilayers, which were identified to be TiO2 according to the XRD result when x > 20 (Fig. 1(b)). It indicated that these surplus Ti3C2 multilayers were not inserted by TAA molecules and susceptible to oxidation. Such phenomenon well explains the presence of TiO2 peak in the XRD patterns (Fig. 1(b)). Even so, the TiO2 nanoparticles only exist on the surface of those surplus multilayered Ti3C2, but did not have any discernible effect on the heterojunction of the ZnIn2S4 and Ti3C2 nanosheets. In addition, it is well known that anatase TiO2 only responds to ultraviolet light in the photocatalysis process. Therefore, TiO2 could be reasonably neglected in the following characterizations and visible-light-driven photoactivity tests over the ZTx samples.

For nanomaterials, the morphological structure usually determines their pore structure and specific surface area, which are two significant influence factors for photocatalytic performance [56]. Thereby, the BET specific surface area (A BET) and pore structure of the obtained samples were determined by the N2 adsorption-desorption measurement. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), both ZnIn2S4 and ZTx exhibit a type-IV isotherm with H2 hysteresis loops according to the Brunauer-Deming-Deming-Teller (BDDT) classification, demonstrating the existence of mesopores with a shape of a narrow ink bottle [57]. The corresponding pore size distribution curves (Fig. 4(b)) further confirmed the presence of mesopores. The specific surface areas of the samples have been summarized in Table 1. In the presence of Ti3C2, the A BET of the sample first increased from 58.4 m2 g − 1 (for bare ZnIn2S4) to 73.2 m2 g − 1 (for ZT10), and then decreased to 60.6 m2 g − 1 (for ZT80). The phenomenon was understandable because the ultrathin Ti3C2 nanosheets in ZT10 could certainly supply more exposed surface area than the Ti3C2 multilayers in ZT80. In a word, the result suggests that the loading of Ti3C2 nanosheets effectively increased the specific surface area of ZnIn2S4, and thereby could provide more reactive sites for photocatalytic H2 evolution.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of the samples was measured via an automatic online trace gas analysis system (Labsolar-6A, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd.) under visible-light irradiation (≥ 420 nm) in the presence of TEOA as the sacrificial agent (Fig. 5 (a)). The concentration of the photocatalyst powder has been optimized to be 1.25 g L − 1 using the multi-channel photochemical reaction system (PCX-50B, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd., Fig. 5(b, c)) [58]. Comparison of the photocatalytic H2 production rates of the prepared samples in Fig. 5(d) and Table 1 shows that the incorporation of a certain amount of Ti3C2 promoted the photoactivity of pristine ZnIn2S4, and the ZT10 sample presented the highest photocatalytic H2 production efficiency (97.9 μmol h − 1), which was 2.7 times higher than bare ZnIn2S4 (36.4 μmol h − 1). The corresponding AQE of the ZT10 sample was 24.2% at 420 nm, and its photocatalytic H2 production rates per mass and per surface area were 978.7 μmol h − 1 g − 1 and 13.4 μmol h − 1 m − 2, respectively. For comparison, physically mixed Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 with a molar ratio of 10% (the pm-ZT10 sample) exhibited a photocatalytic H2 production rate of 57.8 μmol h − 1, which was only 1.6 times higher than that of bare ZnIn2S4 (Fig. S4). It indicated the importance of intimate contact between 2D Ti3C2 and 3D ZnIn2S4. However, further increase in the Ti3C2 content of the ZTx composites resulted in a noticeably declining photocatalytic H2 production activity. It exposed that the photoactivity of the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites has a desirable dependence on the amount of Ti3C2 MXene. The reason will be carefully discussed in the following section.

Meanwhile, photostability is also an important factor in evaluating the performance of a photocatalyst. Therefore, the comparison of the cycling activity tests over the ZT10 and ZnIn2S4 samples was carried out, lasting for four cycles with 3 h in one cycle. Fig. S5(a) shows that the photocatalytic H2 production yield of pure ZnIn2S4 decreased by 39.2% after four cycles, while that of the ZT10 sample decreased only by 20.02%. It indicated that although the stability of the ZT composite was still unsatisfactory due to the instability of MXene in the watery environment, the presence of Ti3C2 could restrain the photocorrosion of ZnIn2S4 to a large extent. The ZT10 photocatalyst was able to continue generating H2 at a constant rate for about 24 h until its deactivation (Fig. S5(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the enhanced photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: To uncover the reasons for the enhanced photocatalytic H2 production activity of the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composites, one must explore the separation and transport behavior of the photoexcited charge carriers in the heterostructures, which can be investigated by photo/electrochemical characterizations including photoluminescence (PL), time-resolved transient photoluminescence (TRPL), transient photocurrent responses (TPR), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests.

PL-based characteristics are conventional methods to discuss charge generation, separation and recombination rates. PL emission spectra would be obtained when the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs happens [18]. Therefore, the intensity of the steady-state PL emission peak can directly reflect the recombination efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers of the ZTx samples. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the ZT10 exhibits an apparently weaker PL emission peak than pristine ZnIn2S4, indicating the lower recombination efficiency of the photogenerated electrons and holes [59]. Besides, time-resolved transient photoluminescence spectra can also provide a reliable basis for the quantitative analysis of the dynamics of photogenerated charge carriers [60]. The emission decay curves of the samples are fitted by biexponential kinetics function, based on the Eqs. (S1) and (S2) in Supporting Information. The calculated average emission lifetime (τAve) could reflect the overall emission decay behavior of the samples [61]. As distinctly shown in Fig. 6(b) and Table S1, the τAve of ZT10 (2.68 ns) is shorter than that of ZnIn2S4 (4.13 ns). Such emission lifetime reduction as well as the photoluminescence quenching (Fig. 6(a)) indicates that the introduction of Ti3C2 provides an additional rapid charge transfer channel for ZnIn2S4 [62]. Accordingly, the heterogeneous interface constructed between ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 effectively inhibits the recombination of photogenerated charges and promotes interfacial charge transfer.

TPR and EIS analyses were carried out to directly evaluate the separation efficiency of the photoinduced electron-hole pairs in the samples [63]. As shown in Fig. 6(c) and 6(d), the photocurrent intensity of the ZT10 sample was apparently higher than ZnIn2S4, which is consistent with the smaller arc radius in the Nyquist plot in the EIS spectra [56]. It is understandable because the close contact between ZnIn2S4 and Ti3C2 was favorable for the charge transfer, which could prolong the lifetime of the photogenerated electrons and promote the photocatalytic reaction rate [44]. However, the photocurrent intensity of the ZT80 sample was even lower than that of ZnIn2S4. It is because superfluous Ti3C2 in the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composite (ZT80) existed as a multilayered form as discovered above (Fig. 3(d)), resulting in loose contact between the two components. In addition, the multilayer with very narrow interlayer spacing was not conducive to charge transfer and separation, which instead possibly became a combination center for the photogenerated charges.

In the heterostructure composites, electronic interaction usually exists between the components due to their intimate contact, which could be evidenced by XPS analysis [22]. Fig. 7 displays the comparison of the high-resolution XPS spectra of the Ti3C2, ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 samples. In the C 1 s XPS spectrum of Ti3C2 (Fig. 7(a)), there are four peaks at 288.9, 285.3, 284.8 and 282.0 eV, which corresponded to C − F, C − O, C − C and C−Ti bonds, respectively [64]. In the Ti 2p XPS spectrum of Ti3C2 (Fig. 7(b)), the peaks at 455.8, 459.8, 456.9, 464.6, and 458.7 eV were attributed to Ti−C 2p 3/2, Ti−C 2p 1/2, Ti−O 2p 3/2, Ti−O 2p 1/2, Ti−F bonds, respectively [65]. In the Zn 2p XPS spectrum of ZnIn2S4, the peaks located at 1045.4 and 1022.3 eV correspond to Zn 2p 1/2 and Zn 2p 3/2, respectively [42]. However, in the XPS spectra of ZT10, the C−Ti and C − F peaks were almost absent in the C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 7(a)), and the Ti 2p XPS peaks were also very small, only a weak peak located at 454.7 eV could be detected (Fig. 7(b)). It is owing to the low amount and well dispersion of Ti3C2 nanosheets in the composite. Even so, it could be still observed that the Ti 2p peak of ZT10 shifted towards lower binding energy as compared to Ti3C2, whilst the Zn 2p peaks of ZT10 shifted toward higher binding energy (1049.3 and 1026.2 eV for Zn 2p 1/2 and Zn 2p 3/2, respectively) as compared to ZnIn2S4 (Fig. 7(c)). The peak shift indicated a strong electronic interaction between Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 in the composite, where the Ti 2p orbital gained electrons and the Zn 2p orbitals lost electrons [11, 17, 35]. It certified that the electrons transferred from ZnIn2S4 to Ti3C2 in the ZT10 composite, which could lead to the formation of the Schottky junction [43].

Light absorption property is another important factor to influence the photocatalytic performance. The DRS spectra in Fig. 8 (a) reveal that the light absorption edge of the pristine ZnIn2S4 appears at about 525 nm, indicating that it has excellent visible-light absorption characteristics. As the content of Ti3C2 increased, the light absorption intensity of the ZTx composites showed an obvious enhancement in the visible-light region, which is mainly due to the full spectrum absorption of Ti3C2 with a dark color. The Tauc plots of the ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 (Fig. 8(b)) calculated via the Kubelka-Munk method show that the band gap (E g) of ZnIn2S4 is approximately 2.59 eV [66], and the introduction of Ti3C2 did not change the band gap. To reveal the specific band structure of the photocatalysts, Mott-Schottky plots of the ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 samples were tested and the result was shown in Fig. 8(c) and (d). The slopes of all lines show positive values, manifesting that ZnIn2S4 is an n-type semiconductor [67]. Hence, the determined flat-band potentials by Mott-Schottky plots were directly regarded as conduction-band potentials (E CB) [21]. According to the intercept between the tangent lines and the abscissa axis of the Mott-Schottky plots, the E CB potentials of ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 are approximately −1.05 and −0.87 V (vs. Ag/AgCl at pH = 5.45), respectively. Based on the equation E Ag/AgCl = E NHE − 0.059 pH − 0.197, the E CB potentials of ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 are calculated to be −0.53 and −0.35 V vs. normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) at pH = 0, respectively. Considering the band gap obtained from the DRS spectra (Fig. 8(a)), the valence band potentials (E VB) of ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 were 2.06 and 2.24 V vs. NHE at pH = 0, respectively. Obviously, compared with pure ZnIn2S4, the CB and VB edges of the ZT10 sample shifted to a positive direction. It can be ascribed to the electronic interaction between Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 in the composite, which has been verified by the XPS test.

Furthermore, the Fermi levels of Ti3C2 and ZnIn2S4 before and after their contact were figured out by calculating the work function values via the equation W sample= W probe + e × CPDsample [68], where CPDsample is the contact potential difference (CPD) tested by the Kelvin probe. A gold mesh was used as the reference. W probe is the work function of the Au reference (≈ 4.25 eV), and e is the electronic charge. The CPD results in Fig. 8(e) reveals that the W values of the Ti3C2, ZT10 and ZnIn2S4 samples were determined to be 4.65, 4.33 and 4.13 eV, respectively. Therefore, the Fermi energy levels (E F) of the Ti3C2, ZT10 and ZnIn2S4 were estimated to 0.15, −0.17 and −0.37 V vs. NHE (pH = 0), respectively [44]. The result indicates that the E F of the ZT10 sample was more positive than that of ZnIn2S4 and more negative than that of Ti3C2, meaning that the ZnIn2S4 and Ti3C2 intimately contacted with each other, and the electrons migrated from ZnIn2S4 to Ti3C2 across the interface to reach a new Fermi level equilibrium. The deduced charge transfer direction was well consistent with the above XPS results. In addition, Fig. 8(f) compares the overpotentials of ZnIn2S4 and ZT10 speculated by the LSV measurement. The overpotential for ZT10 (−0.24 V) is smaller than ZnIn2S4 (−0.33 V), indicating that the presence of Ti3C2 in the composite was beneficial for the H2 production over ZnIn2S4 [48].

Taking all the above information into consideration, the photocatalytic H2 production mechanism over the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 heterostructure system was proposed. As illustrated in Fig. 9
, prior to the contact, the Fermi level of Ti3C2 was more positive than that of ZnIn2S4. After close contact, to establish a Fermi level equilibrium in the composite, the electrons transferred from ZnIn2S4 to Ti3C2, and a Schottky barrier was formed at the interface, hindering the back transfer of electrons to recombine with holes. When the sample was exposed to visible light (≥ 420 nm), the photogenerated electrons of ZnIn2S4 rapidly transferred onto the surface of Ti3C2 nanosheets, and eventually reduced the adsorbed h+ to be H2. With the assistance of a suitable amount of ultrathin Ti3C2 MXene as the cocatalyst (x ≤ 10 in ZTx samples), the photocatalytic H2-production efficiency of ZnIn2S4 was significantly boosted, which was mainly attributed to (1) the enlarged specific surface area, (2) the improved visible-light absorption property, (3) the decreased overpotential for H2 production, (4) the accelerated interfacial charge transfer efficiency, and (5) the formed Schottky barrier on the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 interface. However, when x ≥ 20, the H2 production activity declined with increasing Ti3C2 content in the ZTx composites. It is because the excessive block-like structured Ti3C2 will cover part of the surface of ZnIn2S4 microflowers and result in lower utilization of light. In addition, the surplus Ti3C2 multilayer with very narrow interlayer spacing was not conducive to the charge transfer and separation, which instead became a combination center for the photogenerated charges from ZnIn2S4. Although some TiO2 nanoparticles were formed on the surface of the Ti3C2 multilayers, it has no contribution to the visible-light-driven H2 production.

In summary, a new approach was exploited to prepare ultrathin Ti3C2 nanosheet decorated ZnIn2S4 microflower composites with no need for pre-stripping the MXene bulk. The trick is using TAA as both an intercalator of the multilayered Ti3C2 and a sulfur source of ZnIn2S4, and the flower-like ZnIn2S4 microspheres could thus grow up between the interlayers of Ti3C2 MXene to peel it off. The ultrathin Ti3C2 MXene not only enhanced the visible-light absorption capacity and the specific surface area of ZnIn2S4, but also facilitated the separation and transfer of photoexcited charge carriers. With the optimal molar percentage of Ti3C2 as 10%, the visible-light-driven H2-production rate of the ZnIn2S4/Ti3C2 composite reached 978.7 μmol h − 1 g − 1 without any noble metal cocatalyst, which was 2.7 times higher than bare ZnIn2S4. This study could shed new light on the design and synthesis of 2D MXene materials and their derivative composites more efficiently and conveniently.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21972171), the Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation, China (2021CFA022) and National Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students, South-Central Minzu University (202110524009).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2021.12.028.
